name: SCWorx Corp.

profile.description: SCWorx Corp. provides cloud-based data management, normalization, and interoperability solutions for healthcare providers in the United States, primarily supporting hospital ERP implementations with item master file repair, charge description master management, and data governance SaaS services. The company has 9 employees, $2.88M in trailing twelve-month revenue, and an accumulated deficit of $35.4M. Its former CEO was indicted by the DOJ for securities fraud related to a fabricated COVID-19 test kit deal in 2020. SCWorx faces Nasdaq delisting for falling below the $1.00 minimum bid price requirement, with the April 2026 compliance deadline having passed.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: SCWorx scores at the floor on innovation. Track record (0/3): no meaningful new products, market pivots, or business model transformations in the last five years — the company has performed the same healthcare data normalization services since inception [1]. AI deployment (0/2): despite appointing a CTO (Anders Ohlsson) with machine learning experience, SCWorx has not shipped any AI-powered products or reported AI-driven savings [2]. Market share (0/2): $2.88M in annual revenue is negligible in the healthcare IT market, where competitors like Veeva and Informatica operate at multi-billion-dollar scale [1]. Pipeline (1/2): the company has signed new SaaS agreements including a Southeastern healthcare IDN and Midwest academic hospital, and renewed an existing contract at 113% of prior value, providing near-term revenue visibility [1]. R&D intensity (0/1): with 9 employees and ongoing operating losses, there is no meaningful R&D investment [1].

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission floor (1/4): SCWorx's website states a generic purpose around improving healthcare data quality and interoperability — a legitimate area but the statement is boilerplate and interchangeable with any healthcare IT vendor [2]. Community impact (0/2): at 9 employees and a $395K market cap, there are no corporate philanthropy or community programs [1]. Employee retention (0/2): no retention data available; the former CEO's DOJ indictment for securities fraud [3], Nasdaq delisting crisis, and stock decline from an all-time high of $1,559 to $0.37 create extreme organizational instability [1]. Insider buying (0/2): no insider buying cited; given financial distress and legal overhang, insider investment is absent [1].

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: WORX fails the liquidity floor: stock price of $0.37 x 312,751 avg daily volume = approximately $115,718 — far below the $1,000,000 liquidity threshold. Infrastructure score = 0 [5]. For completeness: ROE of -65.5% (floor, score = 0). Profit margin of -142.7% (floor, score = 0). Revenue growth YoY of 3.4% is modestly positive but moot given the liquidity failure. D/E of 2.1% is technically excellent but irrelevant. Forward P/E and PEG are unavailable due to negative earnings. The company reported an accumulated deficit of $35.4M with $2.88M revenue against $4.44M in net losses for fiscal 2025, and auditors have expressed substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern [1].

ai_resilience.label: HIGH RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: SCWorx's core service — healthcare data normalization, item master file repair, and charge description master management for hospital ERP systems — faces direct AI disruption risk. Large language models and AI-native data cleaning tools are increasingly capable of performing exactly the type of structured data normalization, interoperability mapping, and data governance that SCWorx delivers as a manual, services-based offering [1]. As hospital ERP platforms (e.g., Workday, Oracle Health) embed AI-native data management features, demand for specialized human-led data normalization consultants will decline. The new CTO brings ML expertise but the company has not shipped any AI products [2]. AI Risk Protection: 3/9 (EXPOSED — core service is directly substitutable by AI tools). AI Opportunity: 0/1 (no AI product revenue) [1].

infinite_game.summary: SCWorx is a micro-cap company with 9 employees, a $395K market cap, an accumulated deficit of $35.4M, and a former CEO indicted for securities fraud. The company is focused entirely on short-term survival — Nasdaq delisting compliance, cash preservation, and contract renewals. Simon Sinek's Infinite Game framework requires at minimum the organizational stability and leadership integrity to make long-term bets; SCWorx has neither.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): Improving healthcare data quality is a meaningful goal, and SCWorx's SaaS platform does serve that purpose. However, the cause is shallow — the company's website articulates it generically, leadership does not reference it in strategic decisions, and it has not been tested through multi-decade consistency. At 9 employees and $395K market cap, the company's de facto purpose is survival, not advancing a vision of better healthcare data [2].

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): The organization shows classic low-trust signals. Former CEO Marc Schessel was charged by the SEC and indicted by the DOJ for fabricating a COVID-19 test kit deal that inflated the stock 425% on false information [3]. The stock has declined from an all-time high of $1,559 to $0.37. With 9 employees, Nasdaq delisting looming, and the legal overhang of the former CEO's fraud charges, organizational stability is essentially nonexistent [1][3].

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): SCWorx competes in healthcare data normalization against vastly larger firms — Veeva, Informatica, and in-house teams at major consulting firms. There is no evidence of a worthy-rivals learning posture; no management commentary references learning from competitors or using competitive pressure to improve [1].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (0/2): SCWorx has not demonstrated any meaningful strategic pivots. The company has remained in healthcare data normalization since inception, performing the same core services. The brief foray into COVID-19 test kit distribution was fraudulent rather than a genuine strategic pivot [3]. The company is too small and financially distressed to make transformative bets [1].

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (0/2): No evidence of courageous long-term investment decisions. Current leadership (Tim Hannibal, who succeeded the indicted former CEO) has signed new SaaS agreements while navigating Nasdaq compliance, which demonstrates persistence but is survival behavior, not courage [1][2]. The former CEO's fraud represents the opposite of courageous leadership — a short-term scheme to inflate the stock price at shareholders' expense [3].

investment_thesis: SCWorx's only viable path to shareholder value is acquisition: the company has a working SaaS platform, documented hospital customer relationships with long-term contracts, and a niche in supporting Workday ERP implementations — a growing enterprise market. A larger healthcare IT firm could acquire SCWorx for its customer base, data normalization IP, and recurring SaaS revenue at a premium to the $395K market cap [1]. The appointment of CTO Anders Ohlsson with ML expertise could, if executed, add AI capabilities to the platform and increase its strategic value to a potential acquirer [2].
thesis_continued: The bear case is overwhelming: WORX fails IOWN's basic liquidity threshold by approximately 88% ($116K avg daily value vs. $1M minimum) [5]. The stock trades at $0.37 against a Nasdaq $1.00 minimum bid requirement whose April 2026 compliance deadline has passed [4]. An accumulated deficit of $35.4M dwarfs the $2.88M annual revenue [1]. The former CEO was indicted for securities fraud by the DOJ for fabricating a COVID-19 test kit deal [3]. Net losses widened to $4.44M in fiscal 2025 on $2.88M revenue, and auditors cite substantial doubt about the company's ability to continue as a going concern [1]. This is a company in terminal decline absent an acquisition or capital infusion.
key_catalysts: Reverse stock split to regain Nasdaq compliance — a prerequisite for continued public market access, though reverse splits typically destroy shareholder value in micro-caps
New SaaS customer acquisitions in the Workday implementation support market could accelerate revenue toward a breakeven trajectory
Acquisition offer from a healthcare IT or SaaS consolidator would represent the most likely positive outcome for current shareholders
CTO Anders Ohlsson's ML expertise could enable AI-powered data normalization features, increasing platform differentiation and strategic value
key_risks: Nasdaq delisting: the April 2026 compliance deadline for the $1.00 minimum bid has passed with the stock at $0.37; delisting to OTC markets would make shares nearly untradeable for institutional investors and trigger a liquidity death spiral [4]

Going concern: accumulated deficit of $35.4M, net loss of $4.44M on $2.88M revenue in fiscal 2025, and auditors' substantial doubt about the company's ability to continue as a going concern without additional capital [1]

Legal and reputational overhang: former CEO Marc Schessel was indicted by the DOJ for securities fraud related to the fabricated 2020 COVID-19 test kit deal; SCWorx itself paid $125K in SEC penalties plus $471K in disgorgement — reputational damage persists [3][6]

AI disruption: large language models and AI-native data management tools could commoditize healthcare data normalization, eliminating the need for specialized human-led services like SCWorx's core offering [1]
sources: SCWorx Corp. 10-K Annual Report (FY2025) — revenue of $2,877,629, net loss of $4,444,109, accumulated deficit of $35,420,175, 9 employees, going concern disclosure
SCWorx corporate website and investor relations — scworx.com; management team page; CTO Anders Ohlsson appointment
